title: SEGMENT 13 — Phages as vaccine vehicles: display revisited

lines.text: SEGMENT 13 — Phages as vaccine vehicles: display revisited
We spent the entire phage display lecture on the use of phages to display peptides and proteins on their capsids for the purpose of finding binders.
Here I want to make a related but distinct point: the same display capability can be used to build vaccines.

The basic idea: take a phage — M13 or T7 are the most common, although T4 and lambda also work — and engineer it to display, on its surface, a piece of an antigen from a pathogen you want to vaccinate against.
Could be a fragment of HIV gp120.
Could be a piece of malaria circumsporozoite protein.
Could be a SARS-CoV-2 spike fragment.
The phage particle, with the antigen sticking out from its surface, is then injected as a vaccine.

The immune system sees the antigen displayed on a highly immunogenic, particulate, repetitive viral surface — exactly the kind of presentation immune systems respond strongly to — and develops a robust antibody and T cell response against the antigen.
Phage-displayed vaccines have several appealing properties.
Particulate immunogenicity: a viral particle covered in hundreds or thousands of identical copies of an antigen is a much stronger immunogen than the antigen alone in solution.
Immune systems are tuned to recognize repetitive, ordered arrays of antigen as a danger signal, because that's what viruses look like.
Self-adjuvanting: phages contain their own pathogen-associated molecular patterns — unmethylated CpG DNA, lipopolysaccharide from their host bacteria — that activate innate immune sensors.
You don't always need a separate adjuvant.
Cheap and scalable production: phages grow in E. coli, which is the cheapest expression system on Earth.
You can produce phage vaccines at industrial scale for very low cost per dose.
Stable: phage particles are remarkably hardy, surviving room temperature for long periods, surviving freeze-drying.
This makes them attractive for vaccines in low-resource settings, where cold-chain logistics are a major barrier.
The major phage display platforms for vaccines are M13 and T7.
M13 displays peptides on its surface coat proteins, typically pIII or pVIII, the same proteins we discussed in the phage display lecture.
T7, an icosahedral phage, displays peptides on its capsid protein gp10.
T7 has the advantage of higher copy number per particle — hundreds of antigen copies on a single phage — which makes for stronger immune responses.
T4 has even higher capacity.

Several clinical-stage phage vaccine candidates are in development, against malaria, against opioid addiction (yes, vaccines against drugs of abuse are a real research area), against various pathogens for veterinary use.
None are FDA-approved for human use yet, but the platform is maturing.
There's also a hybrid concept where phages serve as DNA vaccine carriers — the phage capsid carries a DNA payload that, when delivered to mammalian cells, expresses an antigen from inside.
This combines the immunogenicity of phage particles with the antigen-expression flexibility of DNA vaccines.
Still early-stage, but interesting.
